agent_output:
{
  "domain_analysis": {
    "business_context": "DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving many critical business rules undefined. This is an early-stage product concept communicated informally at an all-hands meeting, not a formal specification.",
    "bounded_context": "Pricing",
    "ubiquitous_language": [
      { "term": "DynaPricing", "definition": "The product name for the real-time dynamic delivery pricing system" },
      { "term": "Demand", "definition": "Undefined measure of delivery request volume relative to some baseline — exact metric, time window, and geographic scope are unspecified" },
      { "term": "High Demand", "definition": "A state where demand exceeds a threshold — threshold undefined" },
      { "term": "Low Demand", "definition": "A state where demand falls below a threshold — threshold undefined" },
      { "term": "Guardrails", "definition": "Upper and/or lower bounds on price to prevent extreme pricing — specific bounds undefined" },
      { "term": "Base Price", "definition": "Implied but never stated — the reference price from which adjustments are made" },
      { "term": "Price Adjustment", "definition": "A modification to delivery price triggered by demand conditions — magnitude and calculation method undefined" }
    ]
  },

  "business_rules": [
    {
      "id": "BR-1",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must increase when demand is high relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a pricing policy, not a data integrity constraint. A VP could decide to hold prices flat during high demand for a promotional period. The direction and magnitude of adjustment is a business policy lever.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand is classified as 'high', the computed delivery price must be greater than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-2",
      "rule": "Delivery prices must decrease when demand is low relative to a baseline",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a competitive pricing policy. Leadership could suspend downward adjustments if margins are thin. It is a business strategy choice, not a system integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Given demand is classified as 'low', the computed delivery price must be less than the base price"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-3",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must occur in real time, not on a batch/scheduled basis",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Real-time is a performance/freshness requirement. The business could fall back to periodic repricing without corrupting system state. 'Real time' itself is vague — could mean seconds or minutes.",
      "testable_assertion": "When demand conditions change, the new price must be available to customers within a defined latency SLA (SLA undefined)"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-4",
      "rule": "The pricing system must be fully automated with no manual intervention required for standard price adjustments",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "Automation is an operational preference. Manual override capability is almost certainly needed and does not corrupt the system. A policy decision, not an integrity constraint.",
      "testable_assertion": "Price adjustments triggered by demand changes must be applied without requiring human approval in the standard flow"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-5",
      "rule": "A price ceiling (upper guardrail) must exist to prevent prices from exceeding a maximum threshold",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a hard constraint protecting against reputational damage, regulatory risk, and price gouging. If violated, the system is in a corrupt state — it is charging customers a price the business never authorized. Even the CEO's casual language ('so prices don't go crazy') signals this is non-negotiable. Without a ceiling, an unbounded algorithm could produce absurd prices.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may ever exceed the configured maximum price ceiling, regardless of demand level"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-6",
      "rule": "A price floor (lower guardrail) must exist to prevent prices from falling below a minimum threshold",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A delivery below cost is a system integrity failure — the business is losing money on every transaction. Even during low demand, the price must cover some minimum to prevent unbounded financial loss. A floor also prevents $0 or negative pricing, which would be a data corruption scenario.",
      "testable_assertion": "No computed delivery price may ever fall below the configured minimum price floor, regardless of demand level"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-7",
      "rule": "A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value greater than zero",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical. This is a fundamental data integrity constraint — a delivery costs resources to fulfill and must always have a positive price.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every computed delivery price must be > 0 in the system's currency"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-8",
      "rule": "The pricing algorithm must factor in both competitiveness (market positioning) and profitability (margin preservation) — neither goal may be entirely sacrificed for the other",
      "invariant": false,
      "invariant_justification": "This is a strategic business objective, not a system constraint. The relative weighting of competitiveness vs profitability is a policy knob that leadership will tune over time.",
      "testable_assertion": "The pricing model must accept configurable parameters for both competitive positioning and profit margin targets"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-9",
      "rule": "Price adjustments must be driven by demand signals, not arbitrary or random factors",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "If a price changes without a causal demand signal, the system is behaving non-deterministically and has lost traceability. Every price must be explainable by an input demand state. This is an auditability and correctness invariant.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every price adjustment event must reference the demand signal(s) that caused it"
    },
    {
      "id": "BR-10",
      "rule": "A base price must exist as the reference point from which dynamic adjustments are calculated",
      "invariant": true,
      "invariant_justification": "Without a base price, the system has no anchor for 'up' or 'down'. The concepts of increasing and decreasing price are meaningless without a reference. This is a structural invariant of the pricing model.",
      "testable_assertion": "Every delivery route/type must have a configured base price before dynamic pricing can be applied"
    }
  ],

  "acceptance_criteria": [],

  "edge_cases": [
    { "scenario": "Demand spikes instantaneously (e.g., natural disaster, major event)", "expected_behavior": "Price ceiling guardrail must hard-cap the price. System must not exceed ceiling even under extreme demand. Potential regulatory/PR risk of surge pricing during emergencies is unaddressed." },
    { "scenario": "Demand drops to zero", "expected_behavior": "Price floor guardrail must prevent price from going below minimum. System must handle zero-demand gracefully without division-by-zero or undefined behavior." },
    { "scenario": "Demand oscillates rapidly (flapping)", "expected_behavior": "Prices should not oscillate wildly second-to-second. Some dampening or smoothing mechanism is likely needed, but is not specified." },
    { "scenario": "Two customers request a quote at nearly the same instant during a demand transition", "expected_behavior": "Unclear — should they get the same price or different prices? Race condition risk. Fairness implications." },
    { "scenario": "Guardrail values are misconfigured (floor > ceiling)", "expected_behavior": "System must reject invalid guardrail configurations. This is a startup validation invariant." },
    { "scenario": "Demand data feed becomes unavailable or stale", "expected_behavior": "Unspecified. Should the system fall back to base price? Freeze the last known price? Refuse to quote? This is a critical failure mode not addressed." },
    { "scenario": "Price quoted at booking differs from price at delivery time due to real-time changes", "expected_behavior": "Unspecified. Must define when the price is locked — at quote time, at booking confirmation, or floating until pickup? Major customer trust issue." },
    { "scenario": "Regulatory price gouging laws in certain jurisdictions", "expected_behavior": "Guardrails may need to be jurisdiction-specific. Not addressed at all in the spec." },
    { "scenario": "Competitor drops prices dramatically", "expected_behavior": "The 'competitive' goal is stated but no external market data integration is described. Competitive pricing may require inputs beyond internal demand." }
  ],

  "business_impact": {
    "financial": "Direct revenue impact — dynamic pricing can increase revenue 10-30% during peak demand but risks customer churn if perceived as unfair. Under-pricing during low demand erodes margins. Guardrail misconfiguration could cause either lost revenue (ceiling too low) or reputational damage (ceiling too high).",
    "operational": "Requires real-time demand measurement infrastructure, pricing engine with low-latency computation, guardrail configuration management, and monitoring/alerting for pricing anomalies. Adds significant operational complexity vs static pricing.",
    "user": "Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust and predictability. Price transparency and communication strategy are critical but unaddressed. Customers may game the system by waiting for low-demand periods.",
    "risk": "HIGH — Regulatory risk from surge pricing (price gouging laws in many jurisdictions), reputational risk from perceived unfairness, financial risk from algorithm errors producing extreme prices, competitive risk if pricing is not actually competitive. The vagueness of the spec amplifies all risks.",
    "stakeholders_affected": [
      { "group": "Customers", "impact": "Variable delivery prices — higher during peak, lower during off-peak. Trust and fairness perception is critical. Price-sensitive customers may churn." },
      { "group": "Delivery Drivers/Couriers", "impact": "Demand-based pricing affects order volume distribution. High prices may reduce order volume during peak, smoothing workload. Low prices may increase volume during off-peak, affecting earnings expectations." },
      { "group": "Operations Team", "impact": "Must monitor and maintain a real-time pricing engine. Must configure and tune guardrails. Must respond to pricing anomalies." },
      { "group": "Finance/Revenue Team", "impact": "Revenue becomes less predictable. Margin modeling must account for dynamic pricing. Forecasting complexity increases significantly." },
      { "group": "Legal/Compliance", "impact": "Must review pricing practices for regulatory compliance across all operating jurisdictions. Surge pricing during emergencies is a legal minefield." },
      { "group": "Customer Support", "impact": "Will field complaints about price variability. Need clear policies on price disputes and refunds for perceived overcharges." },
      { "group": "Sales/Marketing", "impact": "Must communicate pricing model to customers. Competitive positioning claims must be substantiated." }
    ]
  },

  "confidence": {
    "level": 30,
    "reasoning": "This is an informal CEO statement at an all-hands meeting, not a specification. Almost every critical business parameter is undefined: what constitutes 'high' or 'low' demand, the adjustment formula, guardrail values, geographic scope, time windows, price lock semantics, fallback behavior, and regulatory constraints. The rules extracted are heavily inferred from domain knowledge rather than stated in the spec. The word 'probably' regarding guardrails is particularly concerning — it suggests even the CEO hasn't committed to this constraint. This spec quality is insufficient to build against.",
    "high_confidence_areas": [
      "Price goes up when demand is high (explicitly stated)",
      "Price goes down when demand is low (explicitly stated)",
      "System is automated (explicitly stated)",
      "Some form of guardrails will exist (stated, though hedged with 'probably')",
      "A positive price is always required (physical constraint of the domain)"
    ],
    "low_confidence_areas": [
      "Guardrail values and whether they are hard invariants or soft targets",
      "Definition and measurement of 'demand' — geographic scope, time window, metric",
      "Price adjustment formula and magnitude",
      "When price is locked for a customer (quote time vs booking vs floating)",
      "Fallback behavior when demand data is unavailable",
      "Regulatory compliance requirements by jurisdiction",
      "Whether 'competitive' means using external market data or just lowering prices during off-peak",
      "Whether guardrails are per-route, per-region, or global"
    ],
    "assumptions": [
      "A base price exists from which adjustments are made — never stated but logically required",
      "Guardrails include both a floor and a ceiling — CEO only said 'so prices don't go crazy' which implies ceiling, floor is inferred from profitability goal",
      "Price adjustments are continuous/proportional, not binary (high/low) — inferred from 'adjust' language",
      "The system operates on internal demand signals, not external competitor pricing data — external data integration is not mentioned",
      "Demand is measured per geographic zone or service area — not stated but standard in logistics",
      "Price is locked at some point during the customer journey — not stated but required for a functional system"
    ]
  },

  "escalations": [
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The definition of 'demand' is completely unspecified. It could mean: orders per hour in a zone, ratio of orders to available couriers, predicted demand from ML models, or manual demand classification. The entire pricing model depends on this definition and it is absent.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Operations Team", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Define demand as order volume per time window per zone", "Define demand as order-to-courier ratio", "Define demand as ML-predicted future volume", "Composite metric combining multiple signals"],
      "recommendation": "Start with order-to-courier ratio as it captures both demand and supply capacity, which is what actually drives delivery cost pressure."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Guardrail specifics are entirely undefined and hedged with 'probably'. No ceiling value, no floor value, no scope (global vs per-route vs per-zone), no indication of whether they are hard limits or soft targets.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["Define global fixed ceiling/floor", "Define percentage-based ceiling/floor relative to base price (e.g., 2x ceiling, 0.5x floor)", "Define jurisdiction-specific guardrails", "Define both hard limits and soft warning thresholds"],
      "recommendation": "Define guardrails as percentage multipliers of base price (e.g., max 2.5x, min 0.7x) per service zone, with hard enforcement. This is standard in ride-share and logistics surge pricing."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "Price lock semantics are unaddressed. When does a dynamically computed price become the customer's price? At quote display? At booking confirmation? Or does it float until pickup? This has massive customer trust and legal implications.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Legal/Compliance", "Customer Support"],
      "options": ["Lock price at quote display with a TTL (e.g., 5 minutes)", "Lock price at booking confirmation", "Lock price at payment", "Display a price range with final price at confirmation"],
      "recommendation": "Lock price at booking confirmation with a short TTL on the displayed quote. This is industry standard and balances real-time pricing with customer fairness."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "No regulatory requirements are mentioned. Dynamic/surge pricing is regulated in many jurisdictions, especially during emergencies or natural disasters. Several US states and countries have price gouging laws that would directly constrain this system.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Legal/Compliance", "Customers", "Executive Leadership"],
      "options": ["Proceed without regulatory review and add compliance later", "Pause feature definition until legal review is complete", "Build with conservative guardrails and jurisdiction-specific override capability"],
      "recommendation": "Legal review must happen before guardrail values are finalized. Build the system with jurisdiction-specific guardrail capability from day one. Do not ship without legal sign-off."
    },
    {
      "type": "missing_context",
      "description": "Failure mode behavior is unspecified. What happens when the demand data feed is stale, unavailable, or corrupted? In a fully automated system, this is a critical design decision with direct financial impact.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Operations Team", "Finance/Revenue Team"],
      "options": ["Fall back to base price", "Freeze last known dynamic price", "Refuse to quote until data is fresh", "Use a configurable staleness threshold with graduated fallback"],
      "recommendation": "Fall back to base price when demand data is stale beyond a configurable threshold. This is the safest default — it reverts to known-good pricing rather than risking stale-data-driven mispricing."
    },
    {
      "type": "ambiguity",
      "description": "The adjustment formula is completely unspecified. Is it linear, exponential, tiered, or ML-driven? The difference between a 10% bump and a 300% surge at 'high demand' is the difference between customer acceptance and revolt.",
      "affected_stakeholders": ["Customers", "Finance/Revenue Team", "Engineering"],
      "options": ["Linear multiplier based on demand ratio", "Tiered pricing with defined demand brackets", "Continuous curve with configurable elasticity", "ML model with historical data training"],
      "recommendation": "Start with tiered pricing (e.g., normal/elevated/high/surge with defined multipliers per tier) for predictability and explainability. Graduate to continuous models after collecting real-world data."
    }
  ],

  "rejection_reasons": []
}
